textobject.phrase: opcgw — ChirpStack to OPC UA Gateway

bookinfo.title: opcgw User Manual
bookinfo.subtitle: ChirpStack LoRaWAN to OPC UA Gateway

legalnotice.para: This work is licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0 license.

revision.revremark: Initial release of opcgw user manual

Comprehensive update covering Epics 2-6: SQLite-backed persistence (WAL mode, connection pool), command queue with delivery confirmation, gRPC pagination, support for all metric types, gateway health metrics in OPC UA, stale-data status codes (Good/Uncertain/Bad), structured logging with microsecond timestamps + correlation IDs, configurable verbosity, performance-budget warnings, and remote-diagnostic operation taxonomy. Configuration chapter rewritten against actual TOML keys; new chapters on Logging & Observability and Operational Monitoring; new appendices for configuration reference, OPC UA address space, and operation names.

v2.0 GA Release. Adds Epic 7 (OPC UA security hardening: three-endpoint matrix, private-key permission enforcement, max_connections session cap, opcua_auth_failed and opcua_session_count_at_limit audit events); Epic 8 (real-time subscriptions with DataChangeFilter, configurable subscription and message-size limits, OPC UA HistoryRead and max_history_data_results_per_node); Epic 9 (embedded Axum Web UI behind HTTP Basic auth, dashboard and live metrics page, configuration hot-reload via SIGHUP, CRUD endpoints for applications, devices, and commands); Epic A (storage payload migration closing issue #108 — payload-bearing MetricType, v007 and v008 SQLite migrations, NaN/Inf poller filter, real measurement values round-tripped end-to-end for the first time). New Installation, Upgrade and Migration chapters; Configuration chapter extended for [opcua].max_connections, [web], and the audit-event reason taxonomy. Docker Hub (docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw) and GHCR mirror (ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw) publishing documented; Dockerfile pinned to ubuntu:24.04 and runs as non-root opcgw user (UID 10001).

Web-first configuration and auto-discovery. Epic C (first-run setup wizard, ChirpStack inventory query layer and web UI pickers, duplicate-prevention validator, inventory drift view, and the TOML-to-SQLite migration of applications, devices, metrics, and commands); Epic D (singleton [global]/[chirpstack]/[opcua]/[web] configuration moved into SQLite with a web editor, and decommissioning of the TOML write-back / SIGHUP hot-reload surface). Configuration now lives in SQLite and is managed from the web UI; config.toml is a one-time bootstrap seed. Schema advanced to v010. Configuration precedence: environment variable > SQLite > config.toml > default.

abstract.para: opcgw is a Rust-based gateway that bridges ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server with OPC UA clients for industrial automation and SCADA systems. It polls device metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals, persists them in a local SQLite database (WAL mode for concurrent reads), exposes them via OPC UA 1.04 with stale-data status codes, real-time subscriptions, and HistoryRead, and accepts downlink commands from OPC UA clients with end-to-end delivery confirmation. An embedded Axum web UI — enabled by default and the primary configuration surface as of v2.1.0 — provides a first-run setup wizard, ChirpStack inventory pickers, a configuration editor, and live status / metric views. Configuration is stored in SQLite; config.toml is a one-time bootstrap seed. As of v2.1.0 the gateway persists real typed measurement payloads end-to-end. This manual provides comprehensive guidance on installation, configuration, operation, observability, troubleshooting, and upgrade / migration.

section.title: What is opcgw?
section.para: opcgw (OPC Gateway) is a middleware application that bridges the gap between LoRaWAN sensor networks and industrial automation systems. It provides bidirectional integration between:

listitem.para: ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server — Manages LoRaWAN devices, gathers uplink metrics from distributed IoT sensors, and accepts downlink commands.
OPC UA Clients — SCADA systems, industrial controllers, and human-machine interfaces that consume metrics in real time and issue commands.

section.para: The gateway continuously polls device metrics from ChirpStack, persists them in a local SQLite database with WAL-mode concurrency, exposes the current values plus gateway health as OPC UA variables, and queues outbound commands for reliable delivery.

listitem.para: Real-time data collection — Polls device metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals and exposes them as boolean, integer, float, and string values.
Persistent storage — Metric values and their history are kept in an embedded database, so data survives restarts and remains available for historical analysis.

Command delivery — Send commands (downlinks) to devices through a queue that validates the parameters and tracks each command from pending, through sent, to confirmed or failed. For confirmed downlinks (for example a valve), confirmed means the device acknowledged receipt; opcgw learns this from ChirpStack's live device-event stream. A command that is never acknowledged within the configurable delivery timeout (command_delivery_timeout_secs, default 60 s) is marked failed. The current status of recent commands is readable from the OPC UA CommandStatusQuery variable.

OPC UA 1.04 server — Exposes every configured metric to SCADA and automation clients, alongside a Gateway folder of health metrics (last poll time, error count, ChirpStack availability). Unencrypted, signed, and signed-and-encrypted endpoints are offered concurrently.
Subscriptions and history — Clients can subscribe to live value changes and read historical values over a configurable time window.
OPC UA security — User/password authentication, a configurable limit on concurrent client sessions, and enforced filesystem permissions on the OPC UA private key.
Embedded web UI (opt-in) — An optional, password-protected web interface for monitoring live status and metric values and for managing applications, devices, and commands. Disabled by default.
Data-freshness indicators — Each value is tagged Good, Uncertain, or Bad based on its age, so operators can see staleness at a glance without parsing timestamps.
Graceful degradation — Keeps operating when ChirpStack is unreachable or individual devices return errors; errors and outages are surfaced in the logs and as OPC UA health metrics.
Structured logging — Detailed, structured log output with configurable verbosity for monitoring and troubleshooting.
Data retention — Historical metrics and command history are pruned automatically according to configurable retention windows.
Graceful shutdown — Ctrl-C (or SIGTERM) initiates an orderly shutdown.

section.title: System Architecture
section.para: opcgw runs as a single process built around a few cooperating components that share one embedded SQLite database. The diagram below shows how data moves between them.

textobject.phrase: ChirpStack feeds the poller, which writes metric values to the SQLite store. The OPC UA server reads values from the store and serves them to SCADA clients. The web UI reads and writes configuration in the store. Commands written by OPC UA clients travel back through the poller to ChirpStack as downlinks.

listitem.para: Poller — Connects to ChirpStack over gRPC, polls device metrics on a fixed cadence, validates each value against its configured type, and writes the results to the store. It tracks connectivity and reports outages.

SQLite store — A single embedded database holds the current metric values, the metric history, and the gateway's own configuration. It survives restarts and is the shared source of truth for every other component.

OPC UA server — Builds its address space from the configured applications, devices, and metrics; serves each value with a freshness status code; supports subscriptions and historical reads; and accepts client writes that become device commands.
Web UI (optional) — A password-protected web interface for first-run setup, configuration, and live monitoring. Enabled by default; it can be turned off.
Command delivery — Writes received over OPC UA are validated, queued, sent to ChirpStack as downlinks, and tracked from pending through sent to confirmed or failed.

section.title: Hardware Requirements
section.para: opcgw is lightweight. Its resource needs scale mainly with the number of variables (metrics) and devices you monitor and how long you retain history, not with a fixed minimum. The guidance below is a starting point; size up for larger fleets.

listitem.para: A modest CPU is enough for small and medium fleets. Add cores when you poll many devices, so polling, serving OPC UA, and pruning run in parallel. The published Docker images are 64-bit (x86-64 and ARM64); a 32-bit target would require building from source.

Scales with the number of variables and devices. A few hundred MB is ample for a small deployment; allow on the order of 2 GB for large fleets (hundreds of devices / thousands of variables). Per-variable overhead is small.

Scales with how many variables you store and how long you keep history. The image plus a small database fit in well under 100 MB; budget around 1 GB or more if you retain a month of history at a short polling interval. Log files add to this depending on the configured verbosity.
Outbound TCP to the ChirpStack gRPC endpoint (typically port 8080) and inbound TCP for OPC UA clients (default port 4840).
OS — Linux (Debian, Ubuntu, CentOS, Alpine), macOS, or Windows with Docker. The reference deployment uses Linux containers.

Runtime — Docker, using the published image (docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw:2.1, mirrored at ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.1; multi-arch linux/amd64 + linux/arm64). See . Building from source is also possible (Rust 1.94.0+ and the protobuf-compiler / libssl-dev / pkg-config packages) for platforms the prebuilt image does not cover.
SQLite — bundled via rusqlite's bundled feature; no separate SQLite installation is needed at runtime. The sqlite3 CLI is recommended on the operator host for the migration pre-flight script ().
ChirpStack — version 4.0 or later with the gRPC API enabled and an API token issued for the target tenant.
Firewall — outbound to ChirpStack (gRPC), inbound to OPC UA clients (port 4840 by default), and inbound to the embedded web UI (default port 8080) if [web].enabled = true.

chapter.title: Installation
chapter.para: Installation is a one-time task: you do it once to get the gateway running and serving OPC UA. Everyday configuration — adding applications, devices, and metrics — is done afterwards from the web UI and is covered separately in .

opcgw is distributed as a Docker image and is installed with Docker Compose. The steps are: create a working directory, write a minimal docker-compose.yml, put your secrets in a .env file, supply a minimal bootstrap configuration, prepare the directories and the OPC UA keys, then start the gateway and verify it.

listitem.para: Docker Engine with the Compose plugin (docker compose). See docker.com.
A reachable ChirpStack server and an API token with permission to list applications/devices and read metrics for your tenant.
Two free host ports: 4840 (OPC UA) and 8080 (web UI).

section.title: The docker-compose.yml file
section.para: Create a working directory and a docker-compose.yml inside it. A minimal file is all you need:
section.programlisting: services: opcgw: image: docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw:2.1 container_name: opcgw restart: unless-stopped env_file: - .env ports: - "4840:4840" # OPC UA - "8080:8080" # web UI volumes: - ./config:/usr/local/bin/config # config.toml + secrets.toml - ./pki:/usr/local/bin/pki # OPC UA certificates - ./data:/usr/local/bin/data # SQLite database (must persist) - ./log:/usr/local/bin/log # log files
section.para: Notes:

listitem.para: env_file: .env loads all environment variables from a single .env file (next section). Nothing is configured inside the compose file itself.
The data volume holds the SQLite database and must persist. Without it, every docker compose down destroys your stored metrics, history, and configuration.
Pin a version tag. There is no :latest tag — use a minor line such as :2.1 (latest patch) or an exact release such as :2.1.0. The image runs as the non-root user opcgw (UID 10001).
To pull from the GitHub Container Registry mirror instead of Docker Hub, change the image to ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.1. Both registries carry identical multi-architecture (amd64/arm64) images.

section.title: Environment variables and the .env file
section.para: All of the gateway's environment configuration lives in one .env file next to docker-compose.yml. The repository ships a .env.example template; copy it and fill in your values:
Two variables are required — they are your secrets, which is why they are supplied as environment variables rather than committed to a file:

The password is used both for the OPC UA user login and for the web UI's login. Every variable uses the OPCGW_ prefix, with __ (double underscore) separating nested configuration keys. Any configuration value can be set this way; the variables most deployments touch (ChirpStack address, OPC UA user name, web-UI enable, log level) are listed in .env.example, and the complete list is in .

warning.title: Keep secrets out of version control
warning.para: Never commit a populated .env or bake tokens into the image. .env is git-ignored; set its mode to 0600. For Kubernetes or a secret manager, inject the same OPCGW_* variables by your platform's mechanism instead of a file.

section.title: The bootstrap configuration file
section.para: opcgw keeps its runtime configuration in its SQLite database and is configured from the web UI. On a fresh install it needs a small config/config.toml to seed the very first boot; after that the database is authoritative and you manage everything from the web UI (see ).
A minimal seed only needs to point the gateway at your ChirpStack server:
section.programlisting:
# config/config.toml — one-time bootstrap seed [chirpstack] server_address = "chirpstack.example.com:8080" tenant_id = "<your-tenant-uuid>"
[opcua] # Auto-generate a self-signed OPC UA keypair on first start. # Set this to false in production and provision your own keypair # (see "OPC UA encryption keys" below). create_sample_keypair = true # OPC UA listens on the standard port 4840 by default. To use a different # port, set host_port here and publish the same port in docker-compose.yml.
section.para: You do not add applications, devices, or metrics here — that is done in the web UI after first start. The secrets stay in .env; do not put the API token or password in this file.

section.title: OPC UA encryption keys
section.para: OPC UA's signed and encrypted endpoints use a certificate and private key (the gateway's "application instance certificate"), kept in the pki/ directory:

listitem.para: pki/own/ — the gateway's own certificate.
pki/private/ — the matching private key. Must be tightly permissioned (see below).
pki/trusted/ — certificates of clients the gateway accepts.
pki/rejected/ — certificates of clients that have been refused. To trust a client, move its certificate from here into trusted/.

section.para: Evaluation — set create_sample_keypair = true (shown above) and the gateway generates a self-signed keypair into pki/own/ and pki/private/ on first start.

Production — set create_sample_keypair = false and provision your own keypair; the gateway refuses to start if the key is missing while the option is off. The certificate must carry the gateway's application URI (the [opcua].application_uri value, default urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway) in its SubjectAltName — OPC UA requires this. Generate one with openssl:

step.para: Create a self-signed certificate carrying the application URI, valid here for five years (adjust the subject to your organisation):
step.screen: openssl req -new -x509 -key pki/private/private.pem \ -out pki/own/cert.pem -days 1825 \ -subj "/CN=Chirpstack OPC UA Gateway/O=Your Organisation" \ -addext "subjectAltName=URI:urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway"

step.para: Convert the certificate to DER — the format opcgw loads ([opcua].certificate_path, own/cert.der):
Lock down the private key (the gateway refuses to start otherwise):

section.para: The first time a client connects, its certificate lands in pki/rejected/; move it to pki/trusted/ to allow that client.

section.title: Preparing directories and starting opcgw
section.para: The container runs as UID 10001 and cannot change ownership of host files, so create the bind-mount directories and set their ownership and the private-key permissions before the first start:
Place your config/config.toml (previous sections) in ./config, then start the gateway:
section.screen: docker compose up -d docker compose logs -f opcgw
section.para: Leave the log tail running to watch the first ChirpStack poll cycle complete.

section.title: Verifying the installation
section.para: A successful start emits a sequence of structured log lines. Confirm the gateway came up:

listitem.para: The OPC UA endpoint is listening:
listitem.screen: nc -z localhost 4840 && echo "OPC UA endpoint reachable"

listitem.para: The startup checkpoints are present (configuration loaded, ChirpStack connected, OPC UA bound):
The first poll cycle has run (usually within ~30 s of start):
If nothing appears after ~30 s, see .
The container runs as the non-root user:
listitem.screen: docker exec opcgw id # Expected: uid=10001(opcgw) gid=10001(opcgw)

listitem.para: The web UI is reachable and requires authentication — browse to http://<host>:8080, which should prompt for the user name (default opcua-user) and the password from your .env. This is where you continue with .

important.para: The web UI is HTTP-only — its credentials travel in cleartext. For anything beyond a trusted LAN, put a TLS-terminating reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) in front of it. See .

section.title: Logging
section.para: Logging is set up once, at install time. The gateway writes structured log lines (microsecond UTC timestamps, key=value fields) to both the container's standard error — visible via docker compose logs — and to daily-rolling files in the log volume (./log on the host). A root log plus per-area files (ChirpStack, OPC UA, storage, configuration) are produced.
Set the verbosity with OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL in .env — one of error, warn, info (default), debug, or trace:
The log files rotate daily; see for retention and housekeeping.

section.title: Stopping the gateway
section.para: Stop opcgw with docker compose stop (keep the container) or docker compose down (remove it; your data volume persists). Both send SIGTERM, which the gateway shuts down on gracefully: it stops accepting new OPC UA connections, lets the in-flight poll cycle finish so no metrics are lost, checkpoints the database, and flushes the logs.

warning.title: Avoid force-killing
warning.para: Do not use kill -9 / SIGKILL. The database is journaled and will recover, but in-flight downlink commands may need manual inspection and buffered log lines can be lost. Always prefer a graceful stop.

section.title: Configuration Overview
section.para: After the first boot, opcgw's configuration lives in its SQLite database and is edited entirely from the web UI — you do not hand-edit files for day-to-day changes. config/config.toml is only a first-boot seed: opcgw reads it once to populate a fresh deployment, then SQLite becomes authoritative. Secrets stay in a separate file and environment variables override everything.
The configuration precedence ordering (highest priority first) is:

listitem.para: Environment variables (OPCGW_ prefix) — always win. See .
The SQLite database — the web-UI-editable runtime config.
config/secrets.toml — secret fields only.
config/config.toml — first-boot seed (lowest priority above hard-coded defaults).
Built-in defaults.

section.para: The rest of this chapter follows the path an operator actually takes: complete the first-run setup wizard, then add applications, devices, and metrics through the web UI pickers, and periodically reconcile drift against ChirpStack. A settings reference for each configuration section follows the workflow, and explains how configuration is stored and migrated.

section.title: First-run setup wizard
section.para: From a fresh installation — where config/config.toml still carries the placeholder ChirpStack token and an empty OPC UA password, and the matching OPCGW_* environment variables are unset — the gateway enters first-run mode. Since Story F-2 (v2.2.x) the wizard collects everything needed for first boot: the ChirpStack connection and the OPC UA password, so you never have to hand-edit a configuration file to get a working gateway. The gateway is in first-run mode whenever either secret is missing:

listitem.para: The OPC UA server binds on its configured endpoint but rejects every authentication attempt. External uptime probes can confirm the gateway is alive, but no SCADA client can connect until setup is complete.
The web UI redirects every request to /setup, a one-shot setup form. The wizard is the only reachable page until completion.

step.para: Open the web UI in a browser; any URL redirects to /setup.
Under ChirpStack connection, enter the server address (a full URL such as http://chirpstack:8080), the tenant ID, and a ChirpStack API token with read access to the tenant's applications and devices.
Under OPC UA server, enter a password and confirmation. The password must be non-empty, have no leading or trailing whitespace, not be a placeholder, and the two fields must match. Submit the form.

On success the two secrets (the ChirpStack API token and the OPC UA password) are saved to config/secrets.toml (owner-only file mode 0600, gitignored) in a single atomic write, the non-secret ChirpStack server address and tenant are saved to the gateway database, and the gateway shuts down gracefully.

Your supervisor (Docker restart policy or systemctl Restart=on-failure) restarts opcgw automatically. On the second boot it picks up the new ChirpStack connection and password, login comes online, the poller connects to ChirpStack, and the OPC UA server accepts authenticated connections.

section.para: What the wizard does not touch. The web-UI login user and password and the log-file location stay in .env by design — the wizard never reads or writes them.

One-shot semantics. Once setup is complete, /setup is no longer reachable. To rotate a secret later, either set OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN / OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD or edit config/secrets.toml and restart.
Trust model. Reach the wizard only over a trusted operator network. For public-internet deployments, set OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD before the first start to skip the wizard entirely.

Wizard scope: password only. The wizard collects only the login password. Everything else (ChirpStack credentials, OPC UA endpoint and PKI directory, login user name) must already be present in config/config.toml or supplied via OPCGW_* environment variables before the gateway starts. The login user name defaults to opcua-user if omitted. The recommended starting point is the shipped config/config.toml: copy it, fill in your ChirpStack and admin credentials, and leave the password unset. A fresh deployment with no applications configured boots fine — you add applications, devices, and metrics from the web UI once the wizard is done.

section.title: Adding applications, devices, and metrics via the web UI
section.para: The /applications.html and /devices-config.html pages offer name-driven pickers instead of the legacy free-form UUID/DevEUI text inputs. The pickers are populated from GET /api/inventory/{applications,devices,uplinks} — the operator never needs to copy a UUID from ChirpStack into opcgw by hand.
The full add-flow is application → device → metric:

listitem.para: On /applications.html, the Create application form shows a dropdown sourced from /api/inventory/applications. Pick an application by name; the hidden application_id pre-fills and the Application Name field defaults to the ChirpStack name (operator-editable). Submit lands the application in config/config.toml through the existing CRUD path.

On /devices-config.html, each already-configured application has an Add device sub-form. The dropdown fetches /api/inventory/devices?application_id=<id> and lets the operator pick a device by name. The DevEUI is shown beneath the picker as a small read-only footnote so the operator can copy it for ChirpStack-side troubleshooting if needed.

The metric-picker sub-form is fed by /api/inventory/uplinks?dev_eui=<eui>&limit=10 and renders the observed_keys aggregate as a multi-select checkbox list. Each row carries an auto-inferred wire-type (Float | Int | Bool | String) which the operator can override per row. Tick any combination of keys to add them as metrics in one submission.

section.para: Manual-entry fallback (load-bearing). Every picker carries a Switch to manual entry toggle. The toggle also fires automatically when ChirpStack is unreachable, so the operator never gets trapped. The chosen mode is remembered per page in the browser, so an operator who prefers manual entry does not have to re-toggle on every page load.
Three failure modes are covered:

listitem.para: ChirpStack maintenance window — picker auto-fallbacks; operator can still configure opcgw using values they know.
Device not yet enrolled in ChirpStack — picker shows an empty-state and flips to manual; the operator can pre-create opcgw config so the device works the moment its first uplink lands.

Metric not yet emitted by the codec — the uplink picker shows no recent uplinks and falls back to manual metric-row entry so the operator can hand-type a metric_name they know the codec will eventually produce.

section.para: Re-fetch on demand. Each picker has a small ↻ refresh icon that re-queries ChirpStack, bypassing the short-lived inventory cache. Useful when you added a device to ChirpStack in another tab and want to see it in opcgw immediately.
Picker activity (opens, manual fallbacks, and the inferred metric type when you add a metric) is recorded in the audit log; see .

section.title: Reconciling drift between opcgw and ChirpStack
section.para: The /inventory-drift.html page compares opcgw's configured applications, devices, and metrics against ChirpStack's current inventory and highlights stale, missing, available, and renamed resources. It covers what happens when the ChirpStack-side inventory changes after opcgw has been configured.
Every resource is classified into one of four buckets:

listitem.para: ok — present in both opcgw and ChirpStack, names match. Information only; no action needed.

stale — configured in opcgw, no longer in ChirpStack (or, for metrics, not seen in the last 10 uplinks). Operator can Remove from opcgw or Keep as alias. Soft-stale metrics (codec emits the key conditionally) are rendered in a softer yellow tone vs hard-stale red.
available — present in ChirpStack, not in opcgw. Add to opcgw deep-links to the picker page with the chosen resource pre-selected.
drifted — present in both but names (applications/devices) or wire types (metrics) do not match. Update opcgw name to ChirpStack's or Keep opcgw alias.

note.title: No background polling
note.para: The drift view is operator-triggered only — opcgw does not periodically compute drift. Every page load (and Refresh now click) forces a fresh fetch from ChirpStack (?refresh=true), bypassing the inventory cache. This bounds the ChirpStack-side load to active reconciliation sessions.

section.para: Typical scenarios:

listitem.para: The device shows as stale. Remove from opcgw deletes the device + its metric mappings (cascade scope shown in the confirmation modal). ChirpStack-side resources are untouched.
The device shows as available. Add to opcgw deep-links to /devices-config.html with the device picker pre-selected; the operator picks metrics from the recent-uplinks list and saves.

The application shows as drifted with both names visible. Update opcgw name to ChirpStack's issues a PUT /api/applications/<id> with the new name; Keep opcgw alias emits a drift_dismissed audit event so the deliberate choice is traceable.

varlistentry.term: Codec firmware adds a metric key

listitem.para: The metric shows as available. Add to opcgw deep-links to the metric picker with the key pre-ticked.

varlistentry.term: Configured wire-type doesn't match observed uplinks

listitem.para: The metric shows as drifted with drift_details.reason="wire_type_mismatch". The drift view links to the devices-config page so the operator can edit the metric type via the canonical edit flow.

section.para: When ChirpStack is unreachable, the page renders the opcgw-side rows as ok placeholders, surfaces a banner explaining the situation, and disables all destructive action buttons + hides Add to opcgw buttons. A Retry button next to the banner re-fires the drift fetch.
Opening the drift view and each reconciliation action you take — including a deliberate Keep as alias decision — are recorded in the audit log; see .

section.title: Settings reference
section.para: The settings below are the gateway's runtime configuration. Normally you edit them from the web UI's configuration editor — that is the supported way to change them, and the values live in the SQLite database. The config.toml snippets shown for each group are only the first-boot seed (and the key names you would use in an OPCGW_ environment-variable override; see ). The [group] names below match those keys and the editor's groups.

section.title: Global settings
section.para: The [global] group currently carries a single flag:
section.programlisting: [global] # Verbose internal debug output (currently a no-op operationally; # reserved for future debug-only behaviours that should not depend on # the tracing log level). debug = true
section.para: All log-related configuration lives under the dedicated [logging] section described next.

section.title: Logging
section.para: Logging is set up once at install time — the log directory and verbosity level. See for setup and for reading the logs in day-to-day operation. The keys are summarised in and .

section.title: ChirpStack connection
section.programlisting:
[chirpstack] # ChirpStack server address (gRPC endpoint, http:// or https://) server_address = "http://chirpstack.example.com:8080"
# API token issued by ChirpStack. NEVER commit a real token to VCS; # use OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN at deploy time. api_token = "your_api_token_here"
# Tenant UUID tenant_id = "ffffffff-ffff-ffff-ffff-ffffffffffff"
# Polling frequency in seconds (default: 10) polling_frequency = 10
# Connection retries when ChirpStack is unreachable (default: 10) retry = 10
# Delay (seconds) between two retries (default: 10) delay = 10
# Page size for pagination in list APIs (default: 100, range 1-1000) list_page_size = 100
# Route ALL devices' values through the uplink event stream (default: false). # See "How device values reach OPC UA" below. #stream_all_devices = false
section.para: How device values reach OPC UA. opcgw has two value paths. The uplink event stream delivers every decoded device report as it happens: OPC UA shows the raw last-known value, stamped with the device's own report time — no averaging or summing (trending is your SCADA's job). The legacy metrics poll instead asks ChirpStack for time-aggregated buckets every polling_frequency seconds; averages and sums corrupt discrete states (a valve status byte must read 195, not an average), so the poll is being retired as a value source. Valve-class devices (any device whose command has command_class = "valve") always use the stream. Set stream_all_devices = true to route every device with read metrics through the stream — the recommended setting. One caveat: fields that ChirpStack sources outside the codec's uplink object (typically batteryLevel from DevStatus) never appear on the stream; if you map one, the log warns with uplink_metric_never_seen. On startup or after a reconnect, opcgw backfills the last value from the device's recent event history, so OPC UA is not empty while waiting for the next report.

warning.title: API Token Security
warning.para: The api_token grants full ChirpStack access. Do not commit production tokens. Override at deploy time:
The token is never written to log output (a security regression test in the suite asserts this).

section.title: OPC UA server
section.programlisting:
[opcua] # Application identity (mandatory) application_name = "Chirpstack OPC UA Gateway" application_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway" product_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway"
# Diagnostics output from the OPC UA stack itself. # REQUIRED true when [opcua].max_connections is set — the session-count # gauge and the at-limit warn both read async-opcua's CurrentSessionCount # diagnostics variable. Startup fail-fasts otherwise. diagnostics_enabled = true
# Optional: TCP hello timeout (default 5 s) # hello_timeout = 10
# Optional: bind address (default: first interface IP) # host_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
# Optional: TCP port (default 4840 for this project) # host_port = 4840
# PKI configuration (relative paths are resolved under pki_dir). # create_sample_keypair = false is the production default. # Set to true ONLY for development self-signed keypair auto-generation. create_sample_keypair = false certificate_path = "own/cert.der" private_key_path = "private/private.pem" trust_client_cert = true check_cert_time = true pki_dir = "./pki"
# User authentication (single user — multi-user RBAC is out of scope). # The password placeholder in the shipped TOML is rejected at startup — # always inject via OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD env var. user_name = "opcua-user" user_password = "REPLACE_ME_WITH_OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD_ENV_VAR"
# Staleness threshold in seconds. # Metrics older than this return Uncertain in OPC UA; # older than 24 h return Bad. Default: 120 (2 x default polling_frequency). # Range: 1-86400. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__STALE_THRESHOLD_SECONDS stale_threshold_seconds = 120
# Maximum concurrent OPC UA sessions. New sessions beyond # this limit are rejected with BadTooManySessions. Existing sessions # unaffected. Default: 10. Range: 1-4096. Setting 0 or > 4096 fails # startup. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__MAX_CONNECTIONS # max_connections = 10
# Subscription / message-size limits. Optional; default to # async-opcua library defaults. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__<UPPER> # max_subscriptions_per_session = 10 # range 1-1000 # max_monitored_items_per_sub = 1000 # range 1-100000 # max_message_size = 327675 # range 1-268435456 (≈ 256 MiB) # max_chunk_count = 5 # range 1-4096
# HistoryRead per-call response cap. Caps the number # of HistoryData rows returned per NodeId per HistoryRead call. SCADA # clients page via follow-up calls (manual paging). Default 10000 ≈ # 28 h of poll data at 10 s polling. Range: 1-1000000. # max_history_data_results_per_node = 10000
section.para: The gateway advertises three OPC UA endpoints on the same TCP port. SCADA clients pick the endpoint matching their security posture; the gateway accepts the user-token credentials against any of the three.

row.entry: Development / first-run smoke tests on trusted LANs.
Signed, unencrypted traffic when LAN traffic must remain inspectable.
Production default. Highest security level the gateway advertises.

section.para: The PKI directory layout (pki/own/, pki/private/, pki/trusted/, pki/rejected/) is documented in docs/security.md § "OPC UA security endpoints and authentication".

listitem.para: Drives the OPC UA StatusCode returned with each metric read. See . May also be overridden per device (see ).

When true, opcgw auto-generates a self-signed certificate on first start. Production default is false — provide real CA-signed (or self-signed) certificates manually at pki/own/cert.der and pki/private/private.pem. Release builds with create_sample_keypair = true emit a startup warning.

Caps concurrent OPC UA sessions. New sessions beyond the cap are rejected with BadTooManySessions; existing sessions unaffected. A misbehaving SCADA reconnect loop or a deliberate session flood cannot exhaust file descriptors or memory. Sized per (N SCADA clients × sessions each) + 20% headroom; values above 50 should prompt a deployment review. Hard cap 4096 (fd-exhaustion guard). Inspect utilisation via event="opcua_session_count" info gauge and at-limit rejections via event="opcua_session_count_at_limit" warns.

Subscription / message-size shaping knobs fed into async-opcua's ServerBuilder at startup. Defaults match async-opcua 0.17.1 library defaults; uncomment only if a deployment scenario requires tuning. Resolved values are echoed at startup as event="opcua_limits_configured".

Issue #155 subscription keep-alive tuning. An idle OPC UA subscription (whose values are not changing) sends a keep-alive every KeepAliveCount × publishingInterval; max_keep_alive_count caps the granted count so lowering it forces keep-alives more frequently — some SCADA clients (e.g. Ignition) otherwise mark slow-cadence tags Uncertain_LastKnownValue. min_publishing_interval_ms raises the floor on the publishing interval (the client still requests the actual rate). Both use a 0 sentinel (= library default) so they are editable as numbers on the web Admin page, and take effect on Apply changes (an in-process soft restart — no container restart; the OPC UA endpoint briefly cycles).

Per-NodeId HistoryRead response cap. SCADA clients exceeding the cap page via follow-up calls (manual paging — continuation points are not yet implemented; see docs/security.md § "Historical data access").

warning.title: OPC UA Private-Key Permissions
warning.para: opcgw enforces filesystem permissions on the OPC UA private key at startup, fail-closed. The gateway refuses to start if any pki/private/*.pem file is not at mode 0o600, or if the parent pki/private directory is not at mode 0o700. The error message includes the observed mode and the chmod recipe. Operator remediation:
warning.screen: chmod 700 pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install
warning.para: Silently running with a world-readable private key is worse than refusing to start — this fail-closed behaviour is intentional.

warning.title: OPC UA Authentication Audit Trail
warning.para: Every failed OPC UA authentication emits a structured warn! event (event="opcua_auth_failed") in log/opc_ua.log. The submitted username is sanitised (control chars escaped, truncated to 64 chars); the attempted password is never logged. Source IP is correlated from async-opcua's info!-level "Accept new connection from ..." line by timestamp — keep OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL at info or below to preserve the correlation, or rely on the per-module opc_ua.log file appender (captures at DEBUG independently).

section.title: Web UI
section.para: opcgw ships an embedded Axum HTTP server gated by HTTP Basic auth. Off by default — existing operators upgrading from earlier releases see no behavioural change unless they explicitly enable it. The credentials are shared with the [opcua] section (same user_name / user_password); one rotation step covers both surfaces.

All configuration and monitoring pages share a single navigation bar across the top of the window (Dashboard, Applications, Devices configuration, Live Metrics, Commands, Inventory drift, Singleton config), so the web UI behaves as one cohesive application. The current page is highlighted in the bar. The web UI is built from plain HTML/CSS/JavaScript with no build step or external framework — a deliberate choice that keeps the gateway lightweight and auditable.
section.programlisting:
[web] # Master switch — default false. # Override: OPCGW_WEB__ENABLED=true enabled = true
# Listening port. Default 8080. Range 1024-65535 (privileged ports rejected). port = 8080
# Bind address. Default "0.0.0.0" (all interfaces). Use "127.0.0.1" if a # reverse proxy on the same host fronts the gateway. bind_address = "0.0.0.0"
# HTTP Basic auth realm string. Default "opcgw". Max 64 chars, ASCII, # no '"', no '\', no leading/trailing whitespace. auth_realm = "opcgw"
# CSRF allow-list for state-changing routes (POST/PUT/DELETE). # Each entry must parse as scheme://host[:port] (no path/query/fragment). # When omitted, defaults to ["http://<bind_address>:<port>"]. # allowed_origins = ["http://gateway.local:8080"]

listitem.para: Live status dashboard. Single-screen view of ChirpStack connection state, last-poll timestamp, cumulative error count, configured application + device counts. Auto-refreshes every 10 s; mobile-responsive layout; OS-driven dark mode. Underlying JSON shape at GET /api/status.

Live metric values page. Per-application / per-device grid showing metric name, typed value (Float / Int / Bool / String — 2.0+), unit, last-updated timestamp, and a staleness badge (Good / Uncertain / Bad / Missing). JSON shape at GET /api/devices.
Minimal smoke endpoint returning {"status":"ok"}. Used by integration tests and external monitoring probes.

POST / PUT / DELETE on the application, device, and command resources. CSRF-protected via [web].allowed_origins. Audit events: application_* / device_* / command_* + *_crud_rejected on validation failure. State-changing mutations also fire topology_change_detected if the OPC UA address space needs reshaping.

section.para: When changes take effect — staged edits and Apply. Every configuration edit you make through the web UI — the application tree (applications, devices, metrics, commands) and the singleton [global] / [chirpstack] / [opcua] / [web] sections — is staged: it is saved to the database but does not change the running gateway yet. A yellow Unapplied configuration changes bar appears at the bottom of the page whenever you have staged edits. When you are done editing, click Apply changes in that bar. The gateway then performs a single graceful in-process soft restart that re-reads the whole configuration from the database and brings the data-plane (ChirpStack poller, OPC UA server, uplink event stream, command handling) back up on the new settings. The Docker container is never restarted by Apply; OPC UA clients briefly disconnect and reconnect once per Apply. Because Apply re-reads everything, all settings now take effect this way — including the network bindings, ports, PKI paths, and OPC UA session limits that previously required a manual restart. The one exception is the web-login credentials (the web server itself survives the soft restart, so rotating user_name / user_password still needs a full process restart). A staged change that fails to load on Apply is non-disruptive: the gateway keeps running the previous configuration and logs apply_failed.

Backing up & restoring configuration (Story F-4). The singleton-configuration page has a Back up & restore section. Export config downloads the whole configuration — the four [global] / [chirpstack] / [opcua] / [web] sections plus the application/device/metric/command tree — as a portable TOML file. Secrets are never included: the ChirpStack API token and the OPC UA password are stripped, so the file is safe to keep in version control or share. Import config uploads a previously exported file: opcgw validates it, merges it over the running configuration (your instance keeps its own secrets — an import never changes them), and stages it. Click Apply changes to activate the imported configuration (one soft restart, as above). Use this to back up a working setup, version it, or reproduce it on another gateway — for example to stand up a demo from a saved file.

warning.title: Web UI is HTTP-only — TLS via reverse proxy
warning.para: opcgw binds an HTTP listener; credentials transit in cleartext. Enable [web] only on trusted LANs. For internet exposure or any path crossing an untrusted hop, deploy a TLS-terminating reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) in front of the gateway and set bind_address = "127.0.0.1" so the listener is loopback-only.

section.title: Command delivery
section.programlisting:
[command_delivery] # Polling interval for ChirpStack delivery confirmations (default 5 s) status_poll_interval_secs = 5
# Maximum time to wait for ChirpStack to confirm a Sent command (default 60 s). # Commands still pending after this become Failed. confirmation_timeout_secs = 60
# Frequency of the timeout-detection scan (default 10 s) timeout_check_interval_secs = 10
# Retention period for completed commands (default 7 days). # Confirmed/Failed commands are purged after this window. history_retention_days = 7

section.title: Applications, devices, and metrics (bootstrap seed)

important.title: Canonical write path: use the web UI, not the TOML file
important.para: The [[application]] tree is stored in SQLite and managed exclusively via the embedded web UI (/applications.html and /devices-config.html). The TOML [[application]] section is read once on the first boot and migrated into SQLite automatically. After migration, editing the TOML file has no effect on the running configuration.

Use this section as a reference when writing an initial bootstrap seed (to pre-populate SQLite on first boot) or when restoring from a TOML backup. For day-to-day configuration changes, use the web UI.

section.para: The [[application]] TOML schema matches the internal ChirpStackApplications struct. Each block declares one application (ChirpStack application UUID + human name) and contains nested device and metric blocks.
section.programlisting:
[[application]] application_id = "ae2012c2-75a1-407d-98ab-1520fb511edf" application_name = "Arrosage"
[[application.device]] device_name = "Niveau_citerne" device_id = "a840418371886840" # DevEUI
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Niveau_cit" # OPC UA browse name chirpstack_metric_name = "Water_deep_cm" # ChirpStack metric key metric_type = "Float" # Bool | Int | Float | String metric_unit = "cm" # optional, web-dashboard suffix
[[application.device]] device_name = "Vanne01" device_id = "524d1e0a02243201"
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Status_v01" chirpstack_metric_name = "valveStatusCode" metric_type = "Int"
[[application.device.command]] command_name = "Open_close_valve01" command_id = 1 command_confirmed = true # request ChirpStack delivery confirmation command_port = 10 # downlink fPort command_class = "valve" # optional device-class binding (Epic E)

listitem.para: application_id must match a real ChirpStack application UUID — opcgw uses it to scope the List calls.
device_id must match the DevEUI of a real device.
[[application.device.read_metric]] declares uplink metrics. The metric_type string drives both the persisted variant in SQLite and the OPC UA Variant projection:

table.title: metric_type to OPC UA Variant mapping

row.entry: Variant::Float(f32) — narrowed at the OPC UA boundary; emits event="metric_read" reason="narrowing_overflow" | "narrowing_underflow" on lossy narrowing
Variant::Int32(i32) when the value fits in i32; Variant::Int64(i64) otherwise (counters, large-magnitude metrics)

listitem.para: metric_unit is an optional human-readable unit string surfaced on the web dashboard as a value suffix (e.g. 34.2 %, 17.5 cm). Empty-string units coalesce to no-suffix.

[[application.device.command]] declares a downlink command surface. OPC UA writes against the corresponding node enqueue a downlink with the configured command_id and command_port; when command_confirmed = true the queue tracks delivery state and times out per the [command_delivery] settings.

The optional command_class binds the command to a device class so opcgw can present a model-independent command surface. When omitted, an OPC UA write is delivered as raw payload bytes (the value is sent verbatim as a single byte). When set to "valve", opcgw maps the canonical value 1 (open) / 0 (close) to a semantic command object ({"command":"open"} / {"command":"close"}) that the ChirpStack device-profile codec encodes into the model-specific wire bytes — so every valve model looks identical on OPC UA. Binding a command to a class also routes the device's values through the uplink event stream (raw last-known value with the device's report time — see the [chirpstack] section), so the valve's status fields always show the true reported state. (Adding new device classes arrives in later Epic E stories; the class is settable from the web command editor.)

note.title: Uniqueness constraints (enforced by validator and SQLite schema)
note.para: application_id must be unique across the application list; device_id must be unique within one application (the same DevEUI under different applications is explicitly allowed); chirpstack_metric_name and metric_name must be unique within one device; command_id and command_name must be unique within one device. These constraints are enforced by both the AppConfig::validate() function and the SQLite composite primary keys (schema v009).

note.title: Audit-event Taxonomy
note.para: Configuration mutations via the web CRUD endpoints emit a closed-enum audit-event taxonomy: application_created / application_updated / application_deleted; device_created / device_updated / device_deleted; command_created / command_updated / command_deleted; config_reload trigger="crud_write" after each successful SQLite commit. See docs/logging.md § "Audit and diagnostic events" for the full reference.

section.title: How configuration is stored and migrated
section.para: Non-secret configuration — the application tree and the singleton sections [global], [chirpstack], [opcua], and [web] — is stored in the SQLite database (data/opcgw.db, file mode 0o600). Edit the application tree on the web CRUD pages and the singleton sections in the singleton-config editor at /singleton-config.html; opcgw reads SQLite on every boot.

Secrets — [chirpstack].api_token and [opcua].user_password — stay in config/secrets.toml (file mode 0o600, written by the first-run wizard). They are deliberately never migrated to SQLite; the singleton editor masks them and refuses to write them.

First-boot migration. On the first boot, opcgw reads config/config.toml and migrates its non-secret values into SQLite. After that, SQLite is authoritative: hand-edits to a migrated key in config.toml have no effect at runtime, and opcgw logs a once-per-boot warning if config.toml is still present alongside a populated database.

note.title: SQLite file permissions
note.para: On fresh creation opcgw sets data/opcgw.db to mode 0o600. Existing databases are not changed retroactively; if the file is wider than 0o600, opcgw warns once per boot. Run chmod 0600 data/opcgw.db on existing deployments.

section.para: Recommended cleanup once the migration has run:

step.para: Verify the database matches your intended configuration:
Optionally archive then remove the seed file:
Restart opcgw. With config.toml absent, the unused-file warning no longer fires and opcgw boots from SQLite + secrets.toml alone.

section.para: To change a runtime setting afterwards, use the singleton-config editor or an OPCGW_ environment-variable override — not a config.toml hand-edit.

section.title: Environment Variable Overrides
section.para: Any configuration value can be overridden by an environment variable, and environment variables always take precedence over SQLite and the seed file. The convention is the OPCGW_ prefix with __ separating nested keys (for example OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__POLLING_FREQUENCY=30). Two convenience aliases, OPCGW_LOG_DIR and OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL, are read early during startup so logging is configured before the rest of the config loads. The full list of variables is in .

section.title: What to Monitor
section.para: The gateway exposes its own health through three channels. For a typical production deployment:

listitem.para: Live health (OPC UA) — read the Gateway folder via OPC UA from your SCADA front-end. See .

Live health (Web UI dashboard) — if [web].enabled = true, browse to http://<gateway-host>:<web-port>/. The landing page (redesigned in Story F-3) leads with an at-a-glance health verdict — a banner that reads All systems operational or a specific degraded reason (ChirpStack unreachable, poller stalled, an apply that failed, devices going stale, or staged changes pending) — and shows tiles for ChirpStack connection state, poller status (active vs stalled, judged against the configured poll interval), last-poll timestamp, cumulative error count, application / device counts, and uptime, plus a device-data-freshness panel (how many devices are fresh / stale / bad / never reported). The verdict and freshness counts are computed in the browser from the gateway's existing status surfaces — opcgw itself computes no new aggregates, and the error figure is a cumulative count since startup (there is no per-error history on this page; use the logs for that). The metrics page at /metrics.html shows live typed values plus a staleness badge (Good / Uncertain / Bad / Missing) per metric. Both pages auto-refresh every 10 s and expose JSON shapes at /api/status and /api/devices for curl | jq / Prometheus textfile exporters.
Diagnostics — tail the per-module log files or aggregate them through your log shipper. See .

section.title: The Gateway Folder in OPC UA
section.para: Under the configured root, opcgw publishes a synthetic Gateway folder containing three health variables:

listitem.para: Time of the last successful poll cycle. The value is NULL (OPC UA Variant::Empty) until the first cycle completes; reading it before that emits a warn log line with warning="no_data_yet".

Cumulative per-cycle error count carried in the persisted gateway status row. Saturates at i32::MAX; a saturation read emits a warning. Spikes between consecutive cycles (delta >= 5) emit operation = "error_spike".
true when the most recent poll cycle succeeded for at least one device, false otherwise. The first per-cycle ChirpStack failure also emits operation = "chirpstack_outage".

section.para: All three reads are subject to the same staleness logic as regular metrics; under normal operation they return Good.

section.title: Stale-Data Status Codes
section.para: Every metric read returns a value plus an OPC UA StatusCode derived from the metric's age relative to [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds. The age is measured from the device's report time (the OPC UA source_timestamp), not from the moment opcgw received the value:

row.entry: Last-known value still served, but stale
Decisively stale; treat the value as suspect

section.para: The global threshold (default 120 s) is often too tight for battery-powered LoRaWAN sensors that report every 15–20 minutes — they would read Uncertain between two perfectly normal reports. Set a per-device override in that case: stale_threshold_seconds = 1800 on the [[application.device]] block (the device-level value wins over [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds). Pick roughly twice the device's report interval.

Transitions between status codes emit operation = "staleness_transition": in-flight transitions at info, the cold-start first observation at debug (so restarts of a large stale fleet do not flood the alert pipeline). Metrics whose age sits within ±5 s of the threshold also emit operation = "staleness_boundary" at debug for diagnosability.

section.title: Source Timestamp Mode (Per Device)
section.para: By default opcgw stamps every served value's OPC UA SourceTimestamp with the device's own report time — the strict OPC UA convention that a tag carries the instant its value was actually produced. For a slow-cadence LoRaWAN device (say a 20-minute uplink interval) that timestamp is legitimately several minutes old and does not advance between uplinks.

Some SCADA clients — notably Ignition — apply their own staleness overlay on top of the value: if the SourceTimestamp is older than a client-side window, they render the tag as Uncertain / Stale, even though opcgw returned Good. The result is that every slow device reads Uncertain between uplinks in the client, and — importantly — raising stale_threshold_seconds does not fix it, because that knob only governs opcgw's own StatusCode, not the client's timestamp interpretation.

The per-device source_timestamp_server flag resolves this. When set to true, opcgw stamps the value with the gateway's current time (now(), the same instant as the ServerTimestamp) so the client always sees a fresh source timestamp and keeps the tag Good. The staleness StatusCode model above is unaffected: a device that genuinely stops reporting past its stale_threshold_seconds still flips to Uncertain, so you do not lose dead-device detection.
Set it in one of three ways:

listitem.para: Web UI — Config → select the device → tick "Use server time as OPC UA source timestamp", then Apply changes.
config.toml — source_timestamp_server = true on the device's [[application.device]] block.
Device CRUD API — the source_timestamp_server boolean field on POST / PUT /api/applications/<app>/devices/<dev>.

section.para: The default (false) preserves strict OPC UA semantics for every existing device; enable the flag only for the devices whose client marks them stale.

listitem.para: Time to complete a full metric poll cycle. Should remain below polling_frequency. Watch the per-cycle duration in the operation = "poll_cycle_end" log line and the budget warning if operation = "batch_write" exceeds 500 ms.
Devices producing successful reads in the most recent cycle. The device_polled success=true count tells you the live fleet; a sudden drop is a signal to investigate ChirpStack or radio coverage.
SQLite database file size growth. The pruner enforces the historical retention window — if the database is growing faster than expected, check the operation = "prune_metric_history" line.

Per-read budget is 100 ms. The gateway emits a warn with exceeded_budget = true on any read past that bound; the microbench measured p95 around 11 µs at 1 kHz read rate, so budget exceedance is a strong signal of contention.
Per-query budget is 10 ms. Persistent SQLITE_BUSY warnings indicate write contention or pool sizing problems.

section.title: Command Queue Monitoring
section.para: Downlink commands traverse three states:

listitem.para: Pending — Enqueued by an OPC UA write; not yet sent to ChirpStack.
Sent — ChirpStack accepted the downlink. If command_confirmed = true, the gateway polls for confirmation every status_poll_interval_secs.
Confirmed / Failed — Terminal states. A Sent command that does not transition within confirmation_timeout_secs becomes Failed.

section.para: Use the SQLite database to inspect queue state directly when needed:
section.screen: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db \ "SELECT command_id, status, created_at, sent_at, confirmed_at, error_message FROM commands ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;"

section.title: Logs and observability
section.para: Every log line is structured — a free-text message plus key=value fields and a microsecond UTC timestamp — so the logs are easy to filter with grep or feed into a log shipper.

Where the logs live. The gateway writes to the container's standard error (visible via docker compose logs) and to daily-rolling files in the log directory (./log by default). A root file captures everything; four per-area files isolate one subsystem each:

listitem.para: opc_ua_gw.log — the root file (everything, at the configured level).
chirpstack.log — ChirpStack polling and connection.
opc_ua.log — the OPC UA server.
storage.log — the SQLite storage layer.
config.log — configuration loading and reload.

section.para: The per-area files always capture full detail regardless of the configured console level, so even when the console is quiet at error you still have a complete per-subsystem trace to investigate after the fact.

Correlation id for reads. Every OPC UA read carries a unique request_id, and all log lines emitted while serving that read — including the storage query and the staleness check — share it. To follow a single client read end-to-end (at debug or lower):

Performance-budget warnings. Three hot paths log a warn with exceeded_budget=true when a single operation runs slower than its budget. In steady state these stay silent; a sustained stream of them points to contention worth investigating.

Audit-relevant and diagnostic log lines carry an event= field for easy filtering (grep 'event="..."' log/*.log); the routine operational lines carry an operation= field. The full catalogues are in and .

section.title: Database maintenance
section.para: SQLite is largely self-maintaining under WAL mode. Two operations are worth scheduling.

step.para: This refreshes query-planner statistics and completes in milliseconds on a production-sized database.
Run the check — expected output is ok:

section.title: Historical metric pruning
section.para: The metric-history store grows with poll volume. A background pruner automatically deletes readings older than the configured retention window, so no operator action is normally required. Pruning activity is logged with operation = "prune_metric_history", including the cutoff time and the number of rows deleted.
There is no explicit "off" switch. To keep history effectively forever, set the retention window longer than your operational horizon.

section.title: Command history retention
section.para: Completed downlink commands (Confirmed or Failed) are retained for [command_delivery].history_retention_days (default 7 days), then purged automatically by the same background pruner.

section.title: Log rotation
section.para: All log files are daily-rolling: at UTC midnight a new file is started with a date suffix. opcgw does not delete old log files — manage retention with your platform's standard tooling (logrotate, journald, Docker's logging driver, or a cron job).

section.title: Symptom Cookbook
section.para: Each subsection below pairs a symptom an operator can observe with the structured log lines that diagnose it and the corrective action.

section.title: Cannot connect to ChirpStack
section.para: Log signature: operation="chirpstack_connect" with success=false, repeated; or a single operation="chirpstack_outage" on the first per-cycle failure.

listitem.para: Confirm the configured server_address resolves and the gRPC port is reachable: nc -vz chirpstack.example.com 8080.

Validate the API token. A 16/Unauthenticated tonic code would surface as a generic OpcGwError::ChirpStack(...); cross-check by hitting ChirpStack directly with grpcurl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ chirpstack:8080 list.

Inspect last_successful_poll in the chirpstack_outage warn line: a long gap indicates a sustained outage. A future release will introduce automatic reconnection; until then, the poller continues attempting on its normal cadence.

section.title: Attempted to add a duplicate
section.para: Log signature: event="application_crud_rejected" or event="device_crud_rejected" or event="command_crud_rejected", each carrying reason="conflict" conflict_kind="duplicate". For a hot-reload (operator hand-edited config/config.toml and saved): event="config_reload_rejected" reason="conflict" conflict_kind="duplicate".

opcgw rejects same-level duplicates server-side: two applications with the same application_id; two devices with the same device_id under one application; two metrics with the same metric_name or chirpstack_metric_name within one device; two commands with the same command_id or command_name within one device.

listitem.para: Find the rejection in the structured log: grep 'conflict_kind="duplicate"' opcgw.log. The field + value + scope fields name exactly what was rejected and at which level.

For an HTTP rejection (web UI / API), the 409 response body has the same shape: {"error":"duplicate","field":"...","value":"...","scope":"...","hint":"..."}. The UI surfaces the hint inline near the offending input — pick a different identifier, or DELETE the existing entry first.

For a config_reload_rejected (TOML hand-edit), the in-memory configuration is unchanged (no partial apply); the on-disk config.toml is also untouched (the gateway does not rewrite operator files). Hand-edit the TOML to fix the duplicate, save the file, and re-trigger the reload (SIGHUP).

Two patterns are explicitly NOT duplicates and remain allowed: the same DevEUI under different applications (one physical sensor exposed under multiple OPC UA namespaces); the same chirpstack_metric_name on different devices (multiple sensors of the same kind).

section.title: Sudden burst of errors
section.para: Log signature: operation="error_spike" with delta >= 5.

listitem.para: Cross-reference the surrounding device_poll warns to identify the affected devices.
If a single fleet-wide gateway, check ChirpStack itself. If a localised group, check the radio path (rssi, snr in ChirpStack UI).
The spike is informational only — it does not gate further polling.

section.title: SQLite busy / locked warnings
section.para: Log signature: operation="storage_query" error="SQLITE_BUSY" sqlite_error_code=DatabaseBusy (or DatabaseLocked).

listitem.para: The sqlite_error_code field distinguishes DatabaseBusy (waiting on a lock) from DatabaseLocked (cross-connection write contention).

WAL mode and the per-task connection pool make rare SQLITE_BUSY tolerable. Sustained busy warnings under steady load suggest pool undersizing or a long-running write — verify the operation = "batch_write" latencies.
Run integrity check during a maintenance window: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;".

section.title: Performance budgets exceeded
section.para: Log signature: any of opc_ua_read, storage_query, or batch_write with exceeded_budget=true.

listitem.para: Capture the request_id on an opc_ua_read warn and grep the file logs for the full read-path trace.
If the bottleneck is storage_query, check query_type and the SQLITE_BUSY rate at the same time.
If batch_write is slow, the cumulative per-cycle metric count (metrics_count) plus disk I/O contention are the usual culprits.

section.title: OPC UA client sees NULL LastPollTimestamp
section.para: Log signature: operation="health_metric_read" metric="LastPollTimestamp" warning="no_data_yet"; once-per-process operation="gateway_status_init".

listitem.para: This is normal during startup, before the first poll cycle has completed. The gateway is healthy.
If it persists past one polling interval, the first cycle is failing — see .

Verify the OPC UA server bound successfully — search the log for event="opcua_limits_configured" (emitted once by src/opc_ua.rs after the OPC UA server applies its session / subscription / message-size limits) or the periodic event="opcua_session_count" gauge.
Confirm the port is listening: ss -tlnp sport = :4840 (or netstat -tlnp | grep 4840).
Confirm the firewall permits inbound connections to the configured port.
If PKI is enabled, confirm the client certificate is under pki_dir/trusted/. Rejected certificates land in pki_dir/rejected/; copy them across explicitly to trust them.

section.title: OPC UA clients see Uncertain or Bad status
section.para: Log signature: operation="staleness_transition" status_code=Uncertain (or Bad); possibly operation="staleness_boundary" for metrics flickering near the threshold.

listitem.para: Map the affected metric to its device and check that the device is producing uplinks (ChirpStack UI).
Verify polling_frequency against stale_threshold_seconds: the latter should be at least 2× the former so transient missed cycles do not flap the status.
For a fleet-wide Uncertain wave that resolves on its own, suspect a brief ChirpStack outage; cross-check chirpstack_outage in the same time window.

section.title: Database integrity errors
section.para: Log signature: OpcGwError::Storage with messages mentioning "disk image malformed".

listitem.para: Stop the gateway gracefully (SIGTERM) so the WAL is checkpointed.
Back up the database before any recovery action: cp -p data/opcgw.db data/opcgw.db.backup.
Run the SQLite integrity check: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;". If it returns ok, the issue may have been transient; restart the gateway.
If integrity is broken, the gateway will recreate the schema on next start — but historical metrics are lost. Plan backups accordingly.

section.title: ChirpStack authentication failure
section.para: Symptom: The gateway never logs a successful chirpstack_connect info line; OPC UA reads return the discriminant string or BadDataUnavailable; no poll_cycle_end emissions.
section.screen:
# systemd: journalctl -u opcgw | grep -E 'operation="chirpstack_connect".*(Unauthenticated|PermissionDenied|InvalidArgument)'
# Docker: docker logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep -E 'operation="chirpstack_connect".*(Unauthenticated|PermissionDenied|InvalidArgument)'
section.para: Root cause: Most commonly an unset, rotated, or wrong-tenant OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN. The shipped config/config.toml contains a placeholder token; the gateway refuses to start with the placeholder in place. ChirpStack returns gRPC code 16 / Unauthenticated when the bearer token is invalid.

listitem.para: Confirm the env-var is set in the running process:
listitem.screen: # Docker: docker exec opcgw env | grep -c OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN # Expected: 1 # systemd: sudo systemctl show opcgw -p Environment | grep -c OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN # Inspect the loaded env-file: sudo grep OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN /etc/opcgw/env

listitem.para: Issue a fresh ChirpStack API token (ChirpStack v4 UI → API Keys) scoped to the configured [chirpstack].tenant_id. Verify independently with grpcurl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" chirpstack:8080 list.
Inject the new token via the env-var (NOT into config.toml) and restart the gateway (systemctl restart opcgw / docker compose up -d).

section.title: OPC UA connection refused / BadTooManySessions
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client cannot establish a session; existing sessions continue working (suggests the session cap), or all clients are refused (suggests auth / endpoint mismatch).

listitem.para: event="opcua_session_count_at_limit" warns — the [opcua].max_connections cap (default 10) is saturated; new CreateSession attempts get BadTooManySessions. Cross-reference the source_ip field to identify a runaway reconnect loop or a port-scan.
event="opcua_auth_failed" warns — user-token credentials don't match [opcua].user_name / [opcua].user_password. The user field is sanitised; the attempted password is never logged.

For at-limit rejections: raise [opcua].max_connections per the tuning checklist in (sized as N SCADA clients × sessions each + 20% headroom), then SIGHUP — note that max_connections is restart-required per the hot-reload taxonomy, so a full process restart is needed. Investigate the offending source_ip if the cap rejection is unexpected.

For auth failures: rotate OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD; verify the SCADA client is configured with the matching credential; confirm OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL is info or below so the source-IP correlation ("Accept new connection from ..." line by async-opcua) survives.

section.title: Polled metrics not appearing in OPC UA Read
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client reads a metric NodeId and receives BadDataUnavailable (legacy-row symptom) or the discriminant string "Float" / "Int" (legacy symptom — should NOT occur on v2.0 GA). Web dashboard shows the Missing badge.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_parse"' # Inspect the row directly: sqlite3 ./opcgw.db \ "SELECT device_id, metric_name, value_type, value_real, value_int, value_bool, value_text FROM metric_values WHERE device_id='<eui>' AND metric_name='<name>';"

listitem.para: value_type='legacy' rows surface as BadDataUnavailable for ONE poll interval after a Path-A migration () until the next poll cycle UPSERTs a typed payload.

event="metric_parse" reason="non_finite" or reason="invalid_bool" or reason="int_overflow" — the poller skipped this metric for this cycle (the NaN/Inf filter at the poller boundary). The metric is skipped, not silently coerced.
If value_type is real / int / bool / text but the corresponding typed column is NULL: schema drift — investigate via PRAGMA quick_check;.

For legacy rows: wait one poll interval; the row will be replaced with a typed payload on the next successful poll. Persistent legacy rows past one interval indicate the poller cannot reach the device — cross-check device_polled success=true.

For reason="non_finite": investigate sensor calibration. For reason="invalid_bool": confirm device firmware emits 0.0 or 1.0. For reason="int_overflow": the metric exceeds i64 range — check device firmware emit precision.

section.title: HistoryRead returns empty / BadDataUnavailable
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client issues an OPC UA HistoryRead over a 7-day window and gets fewer rows than expected, or every row carries StatusCode::BadDataUnavailable.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_history_summary"' | tail -10 journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_history_read"' | tail -10
section.para: Root cause: metric_history_summary (trace) is the aggregate-skip telemetry per query_metric_history call; it carries schema_drift_skipped and unparseable_timestamp_skipped counters. Legacy rows in metric_history surface as DataValue { value: None, status: BadDataUnavailable } — they are NOT silently dropped from the stream. Per-row narrowing failures fire metric_history_read with reason ∈ {narrowing_overflow, narrowing_underflow}.

listitem.para: For legacy rows (BadDataUnavailable in the response stream): expected during the 2.0 payload migration window. New rows captured after upgrade carry typed payloads.

For per-call cap saturation: check [opcua].max_history_data_results_per_node (default 10000 ≈ 28 h at 10 s polling). For longer windows, SCADA clients must page via follow-up calls (manual paging — continuation points are not implemented; see docs/security.md § "Historical data access").

section.title: Gateway refuses to start — PKI permissions
section.para: Symptom: opcgw exits at startup with an error message mentioning the private-key file mode or the pki/private directory mode.
section.screen: ls -la pki/private/ # Expected: # drwx------ ... pki/private # -rw------- ... pki/private/private.pem
section.para: Root cause: opcgw enforces the private-key permissions fail-closed at startup. The gateway refuses to start unless each pki/private/*.pem file is mode 0o600 and the parent directory is mode 0o700. The error text includes the observed mode and a chmod recipe.

listitem.para: Tighten the permissions per the error message:
listitem.screen: chmod 700 pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install

listitem.para: For Docker / Compose deployments, the host-side directory must be at the same modes — UID 10001 owns the files and the gateway cannot chmod loose modes (it can only validate them). Apply on the host before docker compose up.
For an upgrade from older deployments (which left private.pem at mode 0o644): run find pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} \; then restart.

section.title: Web UI returns 401 / 404
section.para: Symptom: Browser displays the gateway URL's Basic-auth prompt repeatedly (401), or after a successful auth shows a 404 on /index.html or /metrics.html.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="web_auth_failed"' | tail -10 # Verify the web server actually started: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="web_server_started"'

listitem.para: For 401: the web_auth_failed warn carries a reason field (missing, malformed_scheme, malformed_base64, missing_colon, user_mismatch, password_mismatch). Web credentials are shared with [opcua].user_name / [opcua].user_password — check the env vars.

For 404: the embedded web server resolves static/ relative to the gateway's CWD. For Docker the Dockerfile copies static/ into /usr/local/bin/static; for systemd the unit file must set WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/opcgw with static/ deployed there.
Confirm env vars are set:
listitem.screen: # Docker docker exec opcgw env | grep -E 'OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_NAME|OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD|OPCGW_WEB_' # systemd sudo grep -E 'OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_NAME|OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD|OPCGW_WEB_' /etc/opcgw/env

listitem.para: Confirm [web].enabled = true (or OPCGW_WEB__ENABLED=true) and that event="web_server_started" appears within ~5 s of startup. Absence of that line means the web server is disabled.
For 404: verify static/index.html, static/metrics.html, and the JavaScript files are present in the CWD-relative static/ directory.

section.title: Log files growing too fast
section.para: Symptom: The [logging].dir directory grows by hundreds of MB per day, eventually saturating local disk.

Root cause: opcgw uses daily-rolling file appenders (one root + four per-module). The per-module files always capture at TRACE independently of the global level — this is deliberate (forensic detail when the console is intentionally quiet). Volume scales with poll cadence and OPC UA read rate; 10 devices at 1 Hz polling under a SCADA client actively subscribing typically produces 50-200 MB/day.

listitem.para: Configure your platform's log-retention tooling (logrotate, journald with SystemMaxUse=, Docker's --log-opt max-size=) — opcgw itself does not delete old daily-rolled files.
Reduce the global console verbosity via OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL=warn (the per-module files still capture at TRACE, preserving forensic detail).
If using a custom log4rs.yaml (legacy from pre-tracing days), inspect its rolling_file + size + roller stanza for the rotation window.

chapter.title: Upgrade and Migration
chapter.para: This chapter covers operator-driven upgrade procedures. The canonical, exhaustively-tested runbook for the v2.0-rc → v2.0 GA transition (payload-bearing MetricType; SQLite schema v006 → v008) lives in docs/deployment-guide.md. The summary below is operator-facing; defer to the deployment-guide for the per-line architectural rationale, SLA tests, and the v007 / v008 migration internals.

section.title: Pre-upgrade Checklist
section.para: Before stopping the current (v2.0-rc) gateway:

listitem.para: Take a file-level backup of the SQLite database. The backup is your only rollback path — the migration is one-way. Use the explicit three-path form, never a glob, so unrelated backup files (e.g. opcgw.db.bak.YYYY-MM-DD) are not collected:
Identify your current schema version. Run the bundled pre-flight script:

The script wraps PRAGMA user_version with an operator-friendly recommendation, and pre-checks that the file is actually an opcgw database (has the metric_values and metric_history tables) so you don't accidentally point it at an unrelated SQLite file (Firefox places.sqlite, etc.).
Version 6 — pre-2.0. Migration applies.
Version 7 — interrupted prior upgrade left the database between v007 and v008. Starting the v2.0 gateway will complete the migration (idempotent — only v008 will be re-applied).
Version 8 — already migrated. No action needed.

Decide Path A vs Path B. Path A preserves legacy (pre-2.0) historical rows (surfacing them as BadDataUnavailable for one poll interval); Path B drops the database file and starts fresh. For most production deployments Path A is the right choice; test / dev gateways and deployments where legacy history is uninteresting can use Path B.

section.title: Path A: In-Place Auto-Migration (Default)
section.para: The recommended path for operators who want to preserve historical metric rows captured under the v2.0-rc schema.

listitem.para: Stop the v2.0-rc gateway gracefully:
listitem.screen:
